questions.optionB: Acid-fast staining (Ziehl-Neelsen — red bacilli on blue background), slow growth on Löwenstein-Jensen (LJ) medium
questions.explanation: M. tuberculosis: acid-fast bacilli (AFB) — due to mycolic acid in cell wall (60% lipid content). ZN stain: red bacilli against blue background (carbol fuchsin, acid-alcohol decolorization, methylene blue counterstain). Slow-growing (generation time ~15-20 hours). Culture: LJ medium (egg-based — buff, rough, tough, dry colonies in 2-8 weeks), BACTEC/MGIT (rapid liquid culture — detects growth in 1-2 weeks). Niacin test positive (differentiates from other mycobacteria). Cord factor (trehalose dimycolate): virulence factor. Reference: Ananthanarayan — M. tuberculosis.

questions.questionText: M. leprae (Hansen's bacillus) causes two polar forms of leprosy:
questions.optionB: Tuberculoid (paucibacillary — strong CMI, few bacilli, granulomas) and Lepromatous (multibacillary — weak CMI, many bacilli, foamy macrophages)
questions.explanation: M. leprae: obligate intracellular, CANNOT be cultured on artificial media. Grows in armadillos (nine-banded) and mouse foot pad. Ridley-Jopling spectrum: Tuberculoid (TT): strong Th1/CMI → few bacilli (paucibacillary), well-formed granulomas, skin patches with loss of sensation (nerve involvement). Lepromatous (LL): weak CMI, strong humoral → many bacilli (multibacillary), globi (clumps of AFB), foamy macrophages (Virchow/lepra cells), leonine facies. WHO MDT: PB (dapsone + rifampin × 6 months), MB (dapsone + rifampin + clofazimine × 12 months). Reference: Ananthanarayan — M. leprae.

questions.questionText: GeneXpert MTB/RIF is a rapid molecular test for TB that detects:
questions.optionB: M. tuberculosis DNA AND rifampin resistance simultaneously (automated real-time PCR — results in 2 hours)
questions.explanation: GeneXpert MTB/RIF (Xpert): automated, cartridge-based nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT/real-time PCR). Detects: (1) M. tuberculosis complex DNA, (2) Rifampin resistance (rpoB gene mutations) simultaneously. Results in ~2 hours. WHO-endorsed as initial diagnostic test. High sensitivity (especially pulmonary TB — 98% in smear-positive, 67% in smear-negative). Sputum sample. Replaced smear microscopy as first-line in many settings. Xpert Ultra: improved sensitivity for smear-negative and HIV-associated TB. Reference: Ananthanarayan — GeneXpert.

questions.questionText: BCG vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine prepared from:
questions.optionB: M. bovis (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin strain — attenuated by 230 subcultures)
questions.explanation: BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin): live attenuated strain of M. bovis. Developed by Albert Calmette and Camille Guérin (230 serial passages over 13 years). Given at birth (intradermal, left deltoid). Protects against: severe forms of childhood TB (miliary TB, TB meningitis). Variable protection against pulmonary TB in adults (0-80%). BCG scar: normal. Complications: BCG adenitis (most common), BCG-osis (disseminated — in immunocompromised). Contraindicated: HIV (symptomatic), immunodeficiency. Makes Mantoux test positive. Reference: Ananthanarayan — BCG.

questions.questionText: Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) is an important opportunistic infection in:
questions.optionB: HIV/AIDS patients with CD4 count <50/μL (disseminated disease)
questions.explanation: MAC (M. avium + M. intracellulare): non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM/atypical). Most common NTM causing disease in HIV/AIDS. Disseminated MAC: occurs when CD4 <50/μL. Features: fever, weight loss, diarrhea, hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia. Diagnosis: blood culture (lysis-centrifugation). Treatment: clarithromycin/azithromycin + ethambutol ± rifabutin. Prophylaxis: azithromycin when CD4 <50. In immunocompetent: MAC can cause pulmonary disease (Lady Windermere syndrome — middle lobe/lingula in elderly thin women). Reference: Ananthanarayan — MAC.
